query: effect of clamp application sequence on distal embolisation in open abdominal aortic aneurysm surgery

retrieved_docs.title: Aortofemoral Bypass -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: For an open technique, the patient positioning is supine with arms abducted to 90 degrees. The patient should undergo prepping from the nipples to the knees. The CFA, superficial, and deep femoral arteries bilaterally are isolated through bilateral groin incisions. It is essential to be sure that the distal portions of the vessels are soft, so they are suitable for clamping. Aortic exposure is typically through a midline incision (transperitoneal approach); however, some prefer a retroperitoneal approach or transverse incision. The retroperitoneum is entered, typically infracolically and after the duodenum is mobilized to the right. The aorta gets dissected below the level of the renal arteries. Most try to remain to the right of the inferior mesenteric vein to avoid violation of the left mesocolon. The renal vein can be mobilized by ligation of its tributaries. Exposure of the aorta needs to be taken down to the level of the inferior mesenteric artery. Tunnels within the retroperitoneum, posterior to the ureters are carried down to the groin incisions. On the left side, the tunnel is often brought posterior to the inferior mesenteric artery to help with keeping the graft isolated from the left mesocolon. [17] [21] [16] Heparin is administered (70 to 100 units/kg) with goal ACT 250 to 300 seconds, and then the aorta is clamped below the renal arteries and on the distal portion already exposed. [22] [23] [24] The surgeon divides the aorta and a portion resected proximal to the inferior mesenteric artery. The distal aorta is oversewn. The chosen graft is anastomosed to the proximal aorta, most commonly in an end-to-end fashion using a 3-0 or 4-0 running permanent suture.

retrieved_docs.title: Abdominal Aortic Repair -- Technique or Treatment -- Open aortic repair:
retrieved_docs.content: The open surgical repair aim is replacing the aneurysmal sac with a synthetic graft tube. The procedure is carried out in a sterile environment under general anesthesia. First, the abdominal aorta is exposed most commonly via a midline incision from the xiphoid to the pubis. Alternatively, a transverse subcostal incision below the ribcage can be used, or less commonly, a retroperitoneal approach is chosen. While the lower postoperative incidence of hernia is associated with transverse subcostal incision, midline incision enables access to all abdominal organs with relative ease. Hence the decision about the incision should be driven by surgeon preference and patient factors. Second, a proximal clamp is placed just below the renal arteries. Later, distal clamps are placed on the common iliac arteries. Third, the aneurysmal segment is removed and replaced with a tube graft. Most commonly, textile polyester material, specifically polyethylene terephthalate, is used. It is recommended to anastomose the aortic graft as close as possible to the renal arteries to prevent later aneurysm development in the remaining infrarenal aortic segment. Only in some selected cases, reimplantation of the inferior mesenteric artery into the graft may be performed. It should be considered if insufficient perfusion of pelvic organs or risk for colonic ischemia is suspected. Finally, abdominal wall closure is performed.

retrieved_docs.title: Popliteal Aneurysm Repair(Archived) -- Technique or Treatment -- A doppler is brought onto the field, and flow is checked proximal and distal to the anastomosis and in the foot. Some providers open the aneurysm sac from above and below the knee and tie off any patent branch vessels present to prevent persistent flow into the sac despite exclusion. The wounds are irrigated and closed in multiple layers. Open Repair, Posterior Approach
retrieved_docs.content: The popliteal vein must be carefully mobilized as it is posterior to the artery and swings to the posterolateral side. Dissection of the popliteal artery aneurysm is performed circumferentially to a healthy artery proximally and distally, and vessel loops are applied. The patient is systemically heparinized to an active clotting time above 250 seconds. Vascular clamps are applied proximally and distally, and the aneurysm sac opened on the anterior surface.

retrieved_docs.title: Popliteal Aneurysm Repair(Archived) -- Technique or Treatment -- Open Repair, Medial Approach
retrieved_docs.content: The soleus fascia is incised, the soleus retracted superiorly, and the gastrocnemius retracted inferiorly with a self-retaining retractor. The below-knee popliteal artery is palpated and circumferentially dissected and isolated with vessel loops. A tunnel is created between the 2 heads of the gastrocnemius muscle. The patient is systemically heparinized to an active clotting time above 250 seconds. The vein is brought through the tunnel in a reversed fashion. Vascular clamps are applied to the proximal popliteal artery, and the distal artery is ligated as close to the aneurysm as possible. End-to-end anastomosis is performed with a running 6-0 polypropylene suture.

retrieved_docs.title: Abdominal Aortic Repair -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Outline the different approaches for treating abdominal aortic aneurysms. Review the complications of the different treatment approaches for abdominal aortic aneurysms. Summarize the indications for treating an abdominal aortic aneurysm. Describe the importance of improving care coordination amongst the interdisciplinary team to improve outcomes for patients affected by an abdominal aortic aneurysm. retrieved_docs.title: Abdominal Aorta Aneurysm Repair(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) repair is performed because the mortality rates are as high as 78% following rupture. Open and endovascular surgery are the two approaches utilized for AAA repair. Open repair is accomplished by either accessing the aorta through a transabdominal or retroperitoneal approach. The aorta is then cross-clamped, the aneurysm sac is next opened and graft in, then sewn within the aorta to exclude the aneurysmal portion. Finally, the aneurysm sac is closed over the graft. In contrast, an endovascular aortic repair is typically performed by accessing the femoral artery through either cut-down or percutaneous means. The endograft is then delivered over a wire in a retrograde fashion to the affected aortic segment and deployed with the use of fluoroscopic imaging. Main repair indications include a large aneurysm diameter (5 cm in females, 5.5 cm in males), rapid expansion, symptomatic aneurysm, and frank rupture. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Popliteal Aneurysm Repair(Archived) -- Technique or Treatment -- Open Repair, Medial Approach
retrieved_docs.content: The graft is flushed, and hemostasis is assured. The vascular clamp is then placed distal to the anastomosis. Vascular clamps are placed on the below-knee popliteal artery, ligating the distal artery as close to the aneurysm as possible. An end-to-end anastomosis is performed with running 6-0 polypropylene sutures. Before completing the anastomosis, the artery is back-bled, forward-bled, and flushed with heparinized saline.

retrieved_docs.title: Femoral Aneurysm Repair -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: The following equipment is needed for this procedure: Sterile drapes Vascular clamps Occlusion balloons (in case vascular clamps are unable to control the vessel proximally or if the dissection is too difficult) Fluoroscopic imaging (C-arm) Micropuncture kit, insufflator A .035 wire, 5F or 6F catheter short sheath Heparinized saline Heparin Ultrasound machine Forceps Self-retaining retractors Electrocautery Scalpel Sutures Interposition graft such as dacron Bovine pericardial patch Contrast Lead shielding

retrieved_docs.title: Aortofemoral Bypass -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The limbs of the graft are then flushed with heparinized saline, clamped and passed through the tunnels into the groin incisions. Clamps are applied to the CFA, and an arteriotomy is made, sometimes down to the level of the profunda, thus requiring a profundaplasty. Endarterectomy follows, if necessary. The graft is anastomosed to the CFA in an end-to-side running manner usually with 4-0 or 5-0 permanent suture. Before completion of the anastomosis, usual backbleeding, forward bleeding and flushing are employed. The opposite femoral artery is done similarly. Blood flow is restored to the CFA, then profunda femoris, and lastly the superficial femoral artery. However, before unclamping, anesthesia should be notified because of the expected hypotension with reperfusion. The retroperitoneum should be closed in layers to adequately omit the graft from the GI tract. If unable to sufficiently close the retroperitoneum, then an omental flap should be created. [17] [21] [16] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Peripheral Vascular Bypass -- Technique or Treatment -- Methods
retrieved_docs.content: The limbs of the graft are then flushed with heparinized saline, clamped, and passed through the tunnels into the groin incisions. Clamps are applied to the CFA, and an arteriotomy is made, sometimes down to the level of the profunda, thus requiring a profundaplasty. Endarterectomy follows, if necessary. The graft is anastomosed to the CFA in an end-to-side running manner, usually with a 4-0 or 5-0 permanent suture. Before completion of the anastomosis, usual back bleeding, forward bleeding, and flushing are employed. The opposite femoral artery is addressed similarly. Blood flow is restored to the CFA, then the profunda femoris, and lastly, the superficial femoral artery. However, before unclamping, anesthesia should be notified because of the expected hypotension with reperfusion. The retroperitoneum should be closed in layers to adequately omit the graft from the GI tract. If unable to sufficiently close the retroperitoneum, an omental flap should be created. [23] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Popliteal Aneurysm Repair(Archived) -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Open repair requires the following types of equipment: Operating room suite Personal protective equipment (mask, eye protection, bouffant, sterile gown, sterile gloves) Skin prep Sterile drapes Ultrasound Intravenous heparin Heparinized saline Electrocautery Standard vascular tray including scalpels (11 and 15 number blades), Castroviejo needle driver, tissue forceps, scissors, self-retaining retractors, vessel loops, bulldog clamps, and vascular clamps A combination of suture and ties per surgeon preference (silk, polyglactin, poliglecaprone, and polypropylene) Doppler to assess patency and flow intraoperatively Equipment needed in endovascular repair are:

retrieved_docs.title: Aortic Rupture (Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The most notable progress in the management of aortic rupture has been the widespread adaptation of stent grafting. This method achieves aortic repair through the delivery and placement of endografts through the femoral arteries. These grafts cover the damaged portion of the aorta and prevent further blood loss. Endovascular treatment has become the preferred method for aortic repair based upon improved perioperative morbidity and mortality. Studies have shown a significant reduction in mortality for patients undergoing endovascular repair when compared to open surgery (7.2% versus 23.5%). Further studies have revealed significantly lower postoperative rates of spinal cord ischemia, paraplegia, stroke, and end-stage renal disease in those patients treated endovascularly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 874
retrieved_docs.content: Digital subtraction angiography of the aorta and lower limb performed with up to three unilateral data acquisition runs is primarily associated with the imaging study referred to as صورة الأبهر. The aorta itself has a direct relationship with صورة الأبهر, underscoring its role as the anatomical focus of this angiographic examination. Moreover, the aorta serves as the direct operative site for major vascular interventions such as aortic resection, replacement, or anastomosis. These interconnected concepts emphasize the importance of precise aortic imaging for the planning and execution of surgical procedures on the aorta.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 138
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is a pathological finding in the abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis) that is evaluated using contrast‑based imaging modalities. The primary reference study is the contrast arteriogram of the femoral and lower‑extremity arteries (arteria femoralis), which is classified by the procedure and linked to both contrast aortograms of the abdominal aorta and multiple digital subtraction angiography (DSA) protocols. DSA performed with ≤ 3 unilateral data‑acquisition runs maps to an aortic image, whereas DSA with 3, 4‑6, 7‑9, or ≥ 10 runs—performed bilaterally or unilaterally—are “other_mapped_from” the femoral‑lower‑extremity contrast arteriogram and “primary_mapped_from” the contrast aortogram. This imaging framework supports accurate diagnosis and assessment of disease extent. The abdominal aorta and femoral artery are also central to peripheral vascular bypass surgery, which may be recorded in a patient’s surgical history and associated with postoperative physical‑therapy treatment. Clinical findings such as a thigh bruit (لغط فخذي) are documented at the femoral artery site, emphasizing the need for precise anatomical documentation to guide surgical planning and postoperative care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1162
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is a pathological finding located in the aorta abdominalis. Because disease of the aorta abdominalis can compromise peripheral circulation, it is often associated with the need for a peripheral vascular bypass. The peripheral vascular bypass commonly uses the arteria femoralis as a direct procedural site to create an alternate pathway for blood flow. Consequently, a peripheral vascular bypass constitutes an element of the patient’s surgical history that is relevant to the planning and execution of physical‑therapy‑related treatment, as indicated by the associated record of surgical history linked to physical therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 169
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is a vascular disease localized to the abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis) and contributes to peripheral vascular disease that may require peripheral vascular bypass surgery. Peripheral vascular bypass frequently involves the femoropopliteal artery (arteria femoralis) and can be associated with a femoral bruit, indicating turbulent blood flow. In renal replacement therapy, creation, revision, or removal of an external arteriovenous shunt or fistula for dialysis utilizes veins as the primary procedural site, linking venous access to dialysis and to peripheral vascular bypass procedures. Venous operations on the lower extremity include varicose vein ligation and stripping and stripping of lower‑limb varicose veins, which target the veins of the lower extremity and fall under the broader category of incision, excision, and occlusion of vessels and vascular surgical procedures on the lower limb. Arterial interventions on the lower limb comprise embolectomy and endarterectomy of the femoropopliteal artery performed through leg incisions; these are classified as incision of blood vessels and represent specific types of lower‑limb vascular surgery that regulate arterial blood flow. The interrelated arterial and venous procedures—affecting the abdominal aorta, femoropopliteal artery, and lower‑limb veins—underscore the integrated role of vascular surgery in managing atherosclerotic disease, dialysis access, chronic venous insufficiency, and overall blood‑flow regulation. Documentation of peripheral vascular bypass often includes surgical history and physical‑therapy‑related treatment details, providing comprehensive patient‑care information.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 195
retrieved_docs.content: within a broader cardiovascular hierarchy. Surgical history includes peripheral vascular bypass procedures performed on the abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis) with the femoral artery (arteria femoralis) as the direct site, often documented alongside physical‑therapy‑related surgery and below‑knee amputation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2079
retrieved_docs.content: The aorta abdominalis is directly related to peripheral vascular bypass, serving as the primary inflow source or conduit in surgical procedures that bypass occluded peripheral arteries to restore adequate blood flow. This connection is essential for planning and executing revascularization strategies in patients with peripheral arterial disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1787
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is a disease that manifests in the aorta abdominalis, indicating that the abdominal segment of the aorta is the typical site where atherosclerotic plaques develop. Because the aorta abdominalis is a major conduit for blood flow to the lower extremities, it is also a focal point in the planning and execution of peripheral vascular bypass procedures, which are performed to restore adequate circulation when peripheral arterial disease compromises blood supply. Recognizing the presence of abdominal aortic atherosclerosis and its impact on the aorta abdominalis is therefore essential for clinicians when evaluating patients for peripheral vascular bypass surgery, as it influences both diagnostic assessment and surgical strategy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 193
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is diagnosed by findings in the abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis). The abdominal aorta also serves as the proximal vessel for peripheral vascular bypass, a procedure that may involve the femoral artery (arteria femoralis) as the direct operative site and is associated with thigh bruit (لغط فخذي). Lower‑extremity arterial occlusive disease is treated by embolectomy and endarterectomy of the femoropopliteal artery, which are classified as incisions of blood vessels, a subcategory of incision, excision, and occlusion of vessels, and belong to the broader group of operative procedures on arteries of the extremity and vascular surgical procedures on the lower limb. The therapeutic aim of these arterial interventions is blood flushing (إراقة الدم) to restore flow. In the venous system, the internal rectal venous plexus is the anatomic site of hemorrhoids, described as unspecified hemorrhoids without complications, and is the target of hemorrhoidal ligation

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 257
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is recorded as a pathological finding in the abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis), underscoring the need to image this arterial segment when assessing atherosclerotic disease. In renal replacement therapy, an arteriovenous shunt for dialysis created with an external cannula falls under the procedural category “creation, revision, and removal of arteriovenous fistulas or vessel‑to‑vessel cannulas for dialysis,” which also includes revision of such shunts. Vascular surgical techniques are organized hierarchically: “Incision, excision, and occlusion of vessels” is the parent group for specific actions such as incision of a blood vessel, and it further parents procedures like varicose vein ligation and stripping. Both varicose vein ligation/stripping and arteriovenous fistula creation or revision are performed directly on veins (vény), highlighting veins as the primary anatomic target for many interventions. Peripheral vascular bypass is linked to both

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 145
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is a pathological finding located in the abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis) and underscores the need for imaging of the aorta abdominalis when assessing atherosclerotic disease. The abdominal aorta is also linked to peripheral vascular bypass, for which the femoral artery (arteria femoralis) serves as the direct procedural site; clinical examination of the femoral artery may reveal a thigh bruit (لغط فخذي). In renal replacement therapy, an external arteriovenous shunt created by an external cannula is performed for dialysis (غسيل الكلى) and is classified under the creation, revision, or removal of arteriovenous fistulas or vessel‑to‑vessel cannulas for dialysis, including revision of arteriovenous shunts for renal dialysis. These procedures directly involve veins (vény) and are part of the broader category “incision, excision, and occlusion of vessels.” Lower‑extremity arterial interventions are organized hierarchically: embolectomy and endarterectomy

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1001
retrieved_docs.content: The abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis) is linked to the surgical intervention known as peripheral vascular bypass, indicating that this major vessel may be involved in or affected by the bypass procedure. The femoral artery (arteria femoralis) serves as a key anatomical location for clinical findings, such as a thigh bruit (لغط فخذي), and it is also identified as the direct procedural site where peripheral vascular bypass is performed. In the patient’s medical record, peripheral vascular bypass is recorded as part of the surgical history associated with physical‑therapy treatment, specifying the type of surgery and its timing. Together, these relationships highlight the relevance of the abdominal aorta and femoral artery in planning and documenting peripheral vascular bypass procedures and their integration into broader therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 152
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis, termed تكلس أبهري in Arabic, represents a manifestation of peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis localized to the abdominal segment of the aorta and is associated with an increased risk of ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion, a risk also linked to atrial mucinous tumor (ورم مخاطي أذيني). The aorta serves as the primary anatomic site for peripheral and visceral atherosclerotic disease, functions as the parent vessel for arterial branches, and is the focus of several imaging and surgical interventions. Vascular imaging commonly employs arterial imaging techniques such as arteriografie, which encompasses contrast arteriograms of the femoral and lower‑extremity arteries and contrast aortograms; these are further refined by digital subtraction angiography (DSA) protocols ranging from ≤3 to ≥10 data‑acquisition runs, performed unilaterally or bilaterally, with specific protocols (e.g., bilateral 4‑to‑6‑run) directly mapped from contrast aortograms and the ≤3‑run unilateral DSA mapped to the aortic image (صورة الأبهر). The femoral artery (arteria femoralis) is evaluated with contrast arteriograms, may present a thigh bruit (لغط فخذي) on clinical examination, and serves as a direct procedural site for peripheral vascular bypass surgery. Surgical options for the aorta include resection, replacement, anastomosis, and direct peripheral vascular bypass, with the abdominal aorta specifically related to bypass procedures. Documentation of peripheral vascular bypass appears in the patient’s surgical history and is associated with subsequent physical therapy. Collectively, these anatomical, imaging, and procedural relationships provide a comprehensive framework for assessing and managing vascular disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 184
retrieved_docs.content: and visceral atherosclerosis and is linked to aortic calcification تكلس أبهري, increased risk for ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion, and may be visualized by aortic imaging (صورة الأبهر). The aorta and its abdominal segment (aorta abdominalis) are direct procedural sites for aortic resection, replacement, anastomosis, and peripheral vascular bypass, the latter also involving the femoral artery (arteria femoralis), where a thigh bruit لغط فخذي may be detected on clinical examination. Documentation of peripheral vascular bypass appears in surgical histories and is associated with multidisciplinary care such as physical‑therapy‑related treatment. Collectively, these relationships illustrate the central role of the heart and aorta in rheumatic valve disease, systemic atherosclerotic disease, cerebral perfusion risk, and a range of cardiovascular diagnostic and therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2381
retrieved_docs.content: Digital subtraction angiography of the aorta and lower limb performed with bilateral data acquisition runs of ≤ 3, 4–6, 7–9, or ≥ 10 is primarily mapped from a contrast aortogram, indicating that each of these specific angiographic protocols derives its primary imaging reference from a contrast‑enhanced aortographic study.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 222
retrieved_docs.content: interventions for arterial diseases excluding the aorta (الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالشرايين (باستثناء الأورطي)) comprise the same surgical and endovascular procedures—excision of the carotid artery interior, aneurysm excision, percutaneous coronary intervention and coronary artery grafting—providing clinicians with a classification framework that integrates vascular treatment choices with positional factors affecting blood‑pressure regulation.
